stories.headline: Trump Administration Sues Colorado Over 13-Year-Old Large-Capacity Magazine Ban
stories.summary:
The U.S. Department of Justice filed suit against Colorado on Wednesday seeking to overturn the state's 2013 ban on large-capacity firearm ammunition magazines, which prohibits magazines capable of firing more than 15 rounds without reloading. The lawsuit argues the restriction violates the Second Amendment.

The legal action is part of a broader Trump administration campaign against state-level gun control measures. Earlier this week, the DOJ also sued Denver over its assault weapons ban, and Governor Jared Polis recently signed Senate Bill 25-003, which further restricts semiautomatic weapons in Colorado.

Colorado's magazine restriction has been in place for more than a decade and was enacted in the aftermath of the 2012 Aurora theater shooting. The state is expected to mount a legal defense of the law. The lawsuit adds Colorado to a growing list of states facing federal legal challenges over gun regulations.

stories.headline: Democratic Candidates for Colorado Governor Debate Live Tonight on Denver7
stories.summary:
U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet and Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser will face off in a live debate Thursday evening at 6 p.m. on Denver7, the first televised matchup between the two candidates vying for the Democratic gubernatorial nomination. The debate will also air on KOAA in Colorado Springs and statewide on Colorado Public Radio.

Shannon Ogden of Denver7 will host, with moderation from Denver7's Colette Bordelon, CPR's Ryan Warner, and The Denver Post's Nick Coltrain. A live studio audience will be present. The debate replaces Denver7's regularly scheduled 6 p.m. newscast.

The June 30 primary election will determine the Democratic nominee to succeed Gov. Jared Polis, who is term-limited and cannot seek a third consecutive term. Denver7 will host a Republican gubernatorial debate on May 14, featuring state Sen. Barbara Kirkmeyer and state Rep. Scott Bottoms.

stories.headline: Colorado Lawmakers Kill Bill Requiring Legislative Caucuses to Disclose Donors
stories.summary:
A Colorado Senate committee voted 3-2 Tuesday to kill Senate Bill 168, which would have required legislative caucuses to publicly report their fundraising and spending. The bill was sponsored by state Sen. Mike Weissman and Rep. Yara Zokaie, both Democrats, and grew out of controversy surrounding a Vail hotel retreat hosted by the Colorado Opportunity Caucus.

The Opportunity Caucus, made up of moderate Democrats, has declined to fully disclose who funded the retreat, which included lobbyists. Colorado's Independent Ethics Commission is investigating whether the 16 caucus members who attended violated the state's gift ban. Democratic Sen. William Lindstedt of Broomfield, himself facing an ethics complaint over the retreat, voted against the bill alongside two Republicans.

Lindstedt argued the bill was constitutionally dubious and said he preferred a broader approach through Secretary of State statutes. Republican Sen. Rod Pelton said mandatory public disclosure could have a chilling effect on minority-party lawmakers joining caucuses. The defeat leaves legislative caucus finances largely unregulated.

stories.headline: Denver Bid for 2028 Democratic National Convention Advances as Party Officials Visit City
stories.summary:
Democratic National Committee leaders and a technical advisory group visited Denver this week to evaluate the city as a potential host for the 2028 Democratic National Convention. Denver is among five finalists, along with Atlanta, Boston, Chicago, and Philadelphia. DNC Chair Ken Martin attended a press event at Ball Arena on May 6, noting the convention draws more than 35,000 delegates, guests, and members of the press.

Mayor Mike Johnston said most events would be centered at Ball Arena and the Denver Convention Center, with downtown serving as a walkable convention hub. Unlike the 2008 DNC, there are no plans to use Mile High Stadium. City officials estimate a significantly larger economic impact than the $266 million generated for the metro area in 2008; the 2024 Chicago DNC contributed an estimated $371 million to that city's economy.

If selected, Denver Democrats would need to raise between $75 million and $90 million to host the event, with an estimated 70 percent expected to come from national corporate and individual donors. Denver last hosted a major party convention in 2008, when Barack Obama accepted the Democratic presidential nomination at Invesco Field.

stories.headline: Colorado Voters Increasingly See Budget Crisis, But Partisan Divide Complicates Picture
stories.summary:
A new poll from the nonpartisan Colorado Polling Institute finds that 42 percent of likely Colorado voters now describe the state budget as being in crisis, up sharply from 30 percent at the same point in 2025. The survey of 613 likely voters was conducted in March by Republican firm New Bridge Strategy and Democratic firm Aspect Strategic.

The results reveal a striking partisan split: 55 percent of Republican voters called the budget a crisis, compared to just 27 percent of Democrats — even though Democratic leaders have long argued the state lacks sufficient funding for healthcare and education. Analysts suggest national politics, including high-profile federal spending debates, may be coloring how Republican voters perceive state finances.

The findings were presented at The Colorado Sun's annual SunFest conference at the University of Denver. Pollsters said the divide could complicate Democratic efforts to pass ballot measures this year aimed at raising revenue to address the state budget shortfall, particularly if voters are not directly experiencing cuts to services like Medicaid.

stories.headline: Blackstone Commits $90 Million to Refinance Denver Luxury Apartment Complex
stories.summary:
Private equity giant Blackstone has committed $90 million to refinance Rye SoBo, a 353-unit luxury apartment property near Interstate 25 in south Denver. The complex opened in 2021 and the developers secured the refinancing last month.

The deal signals continued institutional investor interest in Denver's multifamily real estate market despite broader headwinds including rising insurance costs and a softening sales environment. Blackstone is one of the largest real estate investors in the world.

The refinancing comes as Denver's apartment market faces a mix of high supply and moderating rents. The involvement of a major private equity firm in a local residential property reflects ongoing confidence in the long-term fundamentals of the Denver market.

stories.headline: Denver-Area Cryptocurrency Company Signs Deal for Nuclear-Powered Data Centers
stories.summary:
A Denver-area cryptocurrency company has signed an agreement to power its data centers with small modular nuclear reactors, joining a growing number of technology and crypto firms pursuing nuclear energy to meet surging power demands. The company is lining up multiple reactors as part of the deal.

Small modular reactors have attracted significant interest from data center operators as artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency mining drive electricity consumption to record levels. Traditional power grids have struggled to keep pace with the energy requirements of large-scale computing operations.

The deal reflects a broader national trend of tech companies turning to nuclear power as a reliable, low-carbon energy source. The Colorado company's identity and the specific reactor suppliers were not fully detailed in available reporting.

stories.headline: In-N-Out Burger Moving Forward with Two New Colorado Locations in Timnath and Broomfield
stories.summary:
In-N-Out Burger is advancing plans to expand its Colorado footprint with new restaurants in Timnath and Broomfield. The chain this week added an "opening soon" label to a 3,860-square-foot location at 4911 Cima Vista Drive in Timnath, just off Interstate 25 south of Fort Collins in Larimer County, though an exact opening date has not been announced.

The company has also submitted a concept review for a 3,890-square-foot restaurant with a 1,400-square-foot outdoor patio at 4181 W. 120th Ave. in Broomfield. That location still requires a neighborhood meeting, formal review, land use review commission hearing, and City Council approval before it can proceed.

In-N-Out opened its first Colorado location in 2020 and currently operates 14 Front Range stores in cities including Denver, Aurora, Arvada, Lakewood, and Castle Rock. The two new locations, if completed, would bring the chain's Colorado total to 15 or 16 restaurants.

stories.headline: Arapahoe Basin to Close Sunday, Ending Colorado's Ski Season After Storm Delivers Fresh Snow
stories.summary:
Arapahoe Basin will close for the 2025-26 ski season on Sunday, marking the end of Colorado's ski season. The resort received a boost from this week's spring snowstorm, which delivered nine inches of fresh snow as of Wednesday morning, with accumulation continuing throughout the day.

A-Basin had originally planned to close last Sunday after announcing in late April that below-normal snowpack and warm spring temperatures made an extended season impossible. But after 14 inches fell the previous week, resort officials added three bonus days — Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. Two lifts will operate and lift tickets will be priced at $39.

A-Basin is traditionally the last Colorado ski area to close each season; in recent years it has remained open into mid-June. This year's early closure reflects a difficult snowpack season, though the late-season storm provided some relief for skiers hoping to squeeze in final runs. Live music is scheduled in the base area Saturday and Sunday.

stories.headline: May Snowstorm Leaves Trail of Tree Damage Across Denver as Cleanup Crews Mobilize
stories.summary:
Denver residents and city cleanup crews are working to clear damage left by this week's May snowstorm, which snapped and downed trees across the metro area. The heavy, wet spring snow placed significant weight on trees that had already begun leafing out, making them more vulnerable to breakage than during winter storms.

The storm prompted school closures and disrupted travel across the Front Range. Neighborhoods throughout Denver reported downed branches blocking streets and damaging property.

City crews have been deployed to address debris and assess damage to the urban tree canopy. Spring snowstorms with wet, heavy snow are particularly destructive to trees in full leaf and can cause damage that persists for years in affected neighborhoods.

stories.headline: Film on the Rocks Announces 2026 Season at Red Rocks With 'Sinners,' 'The Princess Bride' and 'Jurassic Park'
stories.summary:
Film on the Rocks has announced its 2026 season lineup at Red Rocks Amphitheatre, featuring screenings of the recently released horror film "Sinners" alongside beloved classics including "The Princess Bride" and "Jurassic Park." The outdoor film series has been a popular summer tradition at the iconic Morrison venue.

Red Rocks Amphitheatre, operated by Denver Arts & Venues, draws visitors from across the region for both concert and film programming. The Film on the Rocks series typically runs on select evenings through the summer months.

Full scheduling details and ticket information were not immediately available, but the announcement signals the return of the series for another season at one of Colorado's most recognizable outdoor venues.

stories.headline: Avalanche Eliminate Wild With 9-6 Thriller in Game 1, Then Dominate Game 2 to Take 2-0 Series Lead
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche have taken a commanding 2-0 series lead over the Minnesota Wild in the Western Conference Second Round of the NHL playoffs. Colorado won Game 1 in a high-scoring 9-6 contest despite a injury scare involving defenseman Cale Makar, then followed with a 5-2 victory in Game 2 powered by a three-point night from Nathan MacKinnon.

The back-to-back wins have given the Avalanche a strong position heading into the next games of the series. MacKinnon's dominant performance in Game 2 underscored his status as one of the league's premier playoff performers.

Colorado's offense has been prolific through the first two games, combining for 14 goals. The series continues with Games 3 and 4 scheduled in Minnesota.

joke.joke: Be so good they can't ignore you.

topStory.headline: US-Iran Peace Talks Advance as Markets Surge, Oil Prices Drop on Hopes of Strait of Hormuz Reopening
topStory.summary:
The United States and Iran moved closer Wednesday to a potential agreement to end their ongoing war, with Axios reporting that officials in both Washington and Tehran were drafting a one-page memorandum of understanding to halt hostilities and establish a framework for nuclear negotiations. Iran's Foreign Affairs Ministry confirmed a U.S. proposal was under review and said Tehran would convey its response to Pakistan, which is serving as mediator. An end to the conflict remains uncertain, however, with President Donald Trump cautioning on Truth Social that the war would end and the Strait of Hormuz would reopen only if Iran agreed to his terms, warning that renewed bombing would be "at a much higher level and intensity" if talks failed.

Financial markets responded sharply to reports of a potential deal. The S&P 500 closed up 1.5% and the Nasdaq Composite gained 2%, with both indexes reaching record highs, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose more than 610 points. U.S. crude oil fell as much as 15% during the session before moderating, closing down 7% at $95.08 per barrel, and international Brent crude closed down 7.8% at $101.27 per barrel. Despite Wednesday's declines, oil prices remain more than 65% higher than at the start of the year.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi traveled to Beijing on Wednesday for talks with Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi, his first in-person visit to China since the war began on February 28. Araghchi said Iran seeks "a fair and comprehensive agreement" and reaffirmed bilateral ties with China, while Wang called for the Strait of Hormuz to reopen "as soon as possible" and urged that fighting cease without delay. The visit came one week before President Trump is scheduled to meet Chinese President Xi Jinping in Beijing on May 14 and 15, and analysts said Iran was seeking assurances that China would not make concessions to Washington that could undermine Tehran's position.

The Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately one-fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas supplies pass, has been effectively closed since the war began, driving fuel and fertilizer prices sharply higher and raising fears of a broader global recession. U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio urged Beijing earlier Wednesday to press Iran to lift its blockade of the strait. Iran's navy signaled some flexibility, posting on X that "safe, stable passage" through the strait would be ensured under new protocols, though the full reopening of the waterway remains contingent on a broader agreement between the two countries.
